Threat.value: Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm

Memory.value: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.

Negative Appraisals.value: There is something wrong with me as a person

Maladaptive Strategies.value: ruminate about how to get even with assailant
avoid anything that could be stressful
I dwell on how I used to be before the event

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This directly conflicts with the selected memory items indicating the event cannot be put into words and is remembered mainly as images. Easy verbal narration and inability to verbalize the event are not clinically compatible within the same profile.

vignette:
He is a 20-year-old African man in a relationship who presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms following an encounter with police. Since the event, he has remained on edge and easily unsettled, especially when driving with other people, seeing people in uniform, or coming across media coverage related to police or similar incidents. These situations are followed by a marked sense of danger, with strong waves of fear, horror, anger, guilt, and shame, and he describes nights disrupted by difficulty falling asleep or staying asleep.

His memories of the event are fragmented and intrusive. He says he cannot put the frightening experience into words, and what returns are mostly vivid pictures and images rather than a coherent account. At times, sounds from the incident seem to replay in his mind, making the memory feel immediate rather than distant. These sensory intrusions leave him feeling unsafe and intensify his sense that the world can shift suddenly into threat.

He has developed harsh views of himself in the aftermath, saying that he cannot rely on himself, that there is something wrong with him as a person, and that he is inadequate. These appraisals sit alongside the intrusive recollections and add to his ongoing sense of danger. The memory of the incident also feeds into these negative beliefs, leaving him increasingly self-critical and unsure of his own capacity to cope.

In response, he spends time ruminating about getting even with the assailant, dwells on how he used to be before the event, and avoids anything that feels stressful. He also reports taking too many risks or doing things that could cause him harm. These patterns appear to be attempts to manage the persistent sense of threat, yet they leave him more exhausted and disconnected from his previous self. Overall, his symptoms have taken a toll on his sleep, his sense of safety, and his relationship, with the impact of the trauma remaining central in everyday life.
